results.title: YAGNI (“You Aren’t Gonna Need It,”): helps software engineers build with clarity, not clutter
results.content:
1.   SOLID: SOLID is a set of 5 design principles meant for service development and implementation. Most developers (I would say Good ones) use them as guidelines to implement business services with Object-Oriented languages such as Java/Python. SOLID stands for Single Responsibility Principle, Open/Closed Principle, Liskov Substitution Principle, Interface Segregation Principle and Dependency Inversion Principle. The promise of SOLIDly designed services is to provide good qualitythrough more efficiency, maintainability, dependabilityand Accessibility. [...] 1.   YAGNI (You Aren’t Gonna Need It): A principle of extreme programming (XP) that states a programmer should not add functionality until deemed necessary. It aims to avoid waste by not working on features or code that aren’t needed.
2.   Agile Development: A set of values and principles outlined in the Agile Manifesto that promote flexibility, customer collaboration, and responsiveness to change through iterative development.
3.   KISS: emphasizes the importance of writing code that is easy to read, understand, and modify by others, including future team members who might work on the project. It guides engineers to write clear and concise code, favoring straightforward solutions over more complex ones.

SDP-Software Design Principles: [...] ### Load Balancing

   Without YAGNI: Each service has its own Application Load Balancer (ALB), increasing costs unnecessarily.
   With YAGNI: Use one ALB that routes traffic to all ECS services.
   YAGNI Benefit: Lower AWS costs, easier traffic management.

### Event Queues

   Without YAGNI: Kafka or SQS is used for small internal updates when simple API calls would suffice.
   With YAGNI: Use direct API calls and RDS polling instead of an event-driven queue.
   YAGNI Benefit: Faster processing, lower maintenance, reduced costs.

# YAGNI

# \\YAGNI\\

## What is YAGNI?

YAGNI stands for "You Aren't Gonna Need It." It is a principle of extreme programming (XP) that states a programmer should not add functionality until deemed necessary. The rationale is that writing code that is not needed is a waste of time and effort, increases complexity, and can potentially introduce bugs or be difficult to maintain. It encourages developers to focus on implementing what is immediately required by the project and avoid premature optimization or adding features based on speculation about future needs.

results.title: YAGNI Programming Principle. You Ain’t Gonna Need It! | by Okan Yenigün | Python in Plain English
results.content:
```
# ANTI YAGNI approachclass Task: def __init__(self, name, status, due_date=None, priority=None): self.name = name self.status = status self.due_date = due_date self.priority = priority def __str__(self): return f"{self.name} ({self.status})"task1 = Task("Write a blog post", "To Do")print(task1) # Output: Write a blog post (To Do)
```

Or, you’ve only included the essential attributes (`name` and `status`) in the `Task` class. If you later determine that you need `due_date` or `priority`, you can easily add them to the class without affecting existing functionality. [...] Extreme Programming (XP) is an Agile software development methodology that focuses on enhancing software quality and responsiveness to changing customer requirements.

Developed by Kent Beck, Ward Cunningham, and Ron Jeffries in the 1990s, XP emphasizes collaboration, flexibility, and frequent feedback through short development cycles.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Life cycle of extreme programming. Source

The YAGNI principle is one of the foundational concepts of Extreme Programming. It aligns with XP’s emphasis on simple design and avoiding overengineering by ensuring that developers only write code to meet the requirements of the current iteration.

results.title: How to practice YAGNI principle (You Aren't Gonna Need It) | VT Labs
results.content:
## Collaborate with team

The greatest thing that customers can do during software development is to collaborate closely with the team. This will help ensure that the software meets their needs and expectations. Customers may provide comments, ask questions, and report any problems. Working together, the team and customers can produce a successful product.

It's not a waste of executives' time to get involved in software creation. By lowering risks, spending several hours each month saves hundreds and thousands of dollars over time.

### Don’t Skimp. Lack of technical excellence is not YAGNI. [...] Free code review checklist and best practices

Download

One of the three main development principles is YAGNI, or You Ain't Gonna Need It. The other two are KISS (Keep It Simple, Stupid) and DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself). These principles, much like programming paradigms, provide opportunities for growth by allowing entities to exist without labour.

According to Ron Jeffries, one of the three founders of the Extreme Programming software development methodology and Agile Manifesto:

> Always implement things when you really need them, not when you just assume you need them. - Ron Jeffries

## Why YAGNI principle is this important?
